companyCaseStudy.title: How GitLab Scaled Git RPCs with Gitaly & Praefect — cutting git-op latency ~6x and surviving millions of repos
companyCaseStudy.summary: GitLab tackled a classic scaling bottleneck: Git operations (clones, fetches, pushes) were bogging down the Rails app and the monolithic storage. By extracting Git IO into Gitaly and adding Praefect for routing/replication, GitLab reduced median Git-op latency from ~700–800ms to ~100–150ms, handled hundreds of thousands of RPCs per minute, and enabled multi-region replication for GitLab.com.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — git is not just code: it's IO

Serving Git at scale is mostly a storage and RPC problem, not a web problem. Git operations are chatty, heavy on small-file heuristics and packfile generation. For GitLab.com — hosting millions of repositories and hundreds of thousands of repo operations per minute — letting the Rails web processes do raw Git work became untenable: high latency, frequent timeouts, and noisy CPU+IO spikes.

## The problem

- Monolith: Rails app processes executed Git commands (via shelling out) and accessed repo files directly on shared storage.
- Contention: spikes in clone/fetch activity caused Rails CPU to spike and I/O to block many requests.
- Reliability: a single NFS/storage backend meant a single point of failure and slow cross-region performance.

Concrete symptoms that triggered action:

- Clone and fetch median latencies were in the high hundreds of milliseconds (700–800ms) with heavy tails into multiple seconds for large repos.
- Error rates climbed during peak times as shelled-out Git commands collided with each other.
- Operational complexity: debugging git-shellouts in Rails was slow and fragile.

## The challenge

Git operations are not easily tamed by simple caching. They require:

- Fast, consistent access to repository objects and packs.
- Efficient generation and streaming of packfiles.
- Atomic updates on push.
- Horizontal scalability and HA for reads and writes.

So the team needed a way to:

1. Separate Git heavy-lifting from the web layer.
2. Make repository storage scalable and replicable across regions.
3. Route Git RPCs efficiently (read vs write) and keep semantics correct.

## Solution: Gitaly + Praefect (architecture & decisions)

GitLab built a purpose-built binary service called Gitaly: a gRPC-based Git storage service responsible for serving Git RPCs (upload-pack, receive-pack, object access, etc.). To coordinate multiple Gitaly servers and provide high availability + replication, GitLab built Praefect — a router/proxy/service that implements repository-level routing, replication, and health checks.

High-level architecture:

```mermaid
graph LR
  UserCLI[Developer git clone/push/fetch]
  UserCLI -->|git over HTTP/SSH| GLWeb[GitLab Rails / Auth layer]
  GLWeb -->|auth/grant| Praefect[Praefect Router]
  Praefect -->|gRPC| G1[Gitaly Node A]
  Praefect -->|gRPC| G2[Gitaly Node B]
  G1 --> Storage1[Repository Storage (disk/object)]
  G2 --> Storage2[Repository Storage (disk/object)]
  subgraph Geo
    G1
    G2
  end
```

Key architectural decisions and why they mattered:

- Move Git work out of Rails: Gitaly handles Git plumbing directly, reducing CPU and blocking in Rails processes.
- Use gRPC for efficient, typed RPCs: smaller, faster RPCs compared to shelling out and parsing text.
- Repository-level routing via Praefect: allows reads to be served from replicas while writes are forwarded to primary; Praefect also handles replication job orchestration and split-brain avoidance logic.
- Support for hybrid storage: local disks for hot repos, object storage (S3-compatible) for backups and cold data.
- Health checks and automatic failover: Praefect monitors node health and re-routes traffic to healthy Gitaly nodes.

### Flow of a push (simplified)

1. Developer authenticates with GitLab (Rails) and issues `git push`.
2. Rails consults Praefect to find the current primary Gitaly node for the repo.
3. The client connects to the selected Gitaly via gRPC and performs receive-pack.
4. Gitaly updates repo objects and then triggers asynchronous replication to replicas under Praefect control.

## Results (Before vs After)

| Metric | Before (Rails+shellouts) | After (Gitaly + Praefect) |
|--------|--------------------------:|---------------------------:|
| Median git-op latency (clone/fetch) | ~700–800 ms | ~100–150 ms |
| Tail latency (95th/99th) | multiple seconds | ~300–600 ms |
| Error rate during peaks | noticeably higher (spikes) | reduced ~4x |
| Git RPC throughput | constrained by Rails workers | 2–3x higher (horizontal Gitaly nodes) |
| HA / replication | Single NFS/region, manual | Multi-node, orchestrated replication via Praefect |

> Note: exact numbers depend on repo size and workload mix — GitLab published reductions in latency and operational load on Rails after adopting Gitaly/Praefect.

## Why this worked

- Specialization: A focused service (Gitaly) can be optimized for filesystem and Git-specific workloads (packing heuristics, zero-copy streaming) in ways a generic web process cannot.
- Horizontal scaling: Gitaly nodes are stateless in the RPC layer; repo data can be sharded/replicated and nodes added to scale throughput.
- Separation of concerns: Rails keeps auth, permissions, UI; Gitaly keeps data access — easier to reason about performance and failures.
- Pragmatic replication: Praefect provides repo-level primary/replica semantics instead of a heavyweight distributed filesystem.

## Operational lessons & trade-offs

- Complexity: adding Gitaly + Praefect increases the number of moving parts and requires robust monitoring and health checks.
- Consistency vs Availability: replication is asynchronous; reads from replicas may be slightly stale. GitLab mitigates this by routing critical reads/writes to the primary.
- Backwards compatibility: clients and tooling must remain compatible — gRPC endpoints and protocol changes are managed carefully.

## Example Praefect config (sketch)

A simplified Praefect repository routing snippet (YAML-like for illustration):

```yaml
praefect:
  storages:
    - name: gitaly-1
      nodes:
        - address: gitaly-1.internal:8075
    - name: gitaly-2
      nodes:
        - address: gitaly-2.internal:8075
  replication: async
  routing_policy: primary_on_write
```

## Key operational metrics to watch

- Git RPC latency distribution (p50/p95/p99).
- Gitaly node CPU and disk IO (packfile generation is CPU+IO heavy).
- Replication lag per repository and failed replication count.
- Number of active repositories per storage node and per-disk utilization.

## Final note

Gitaly+Praefect is a concrete example of "move the heavy stateful work out of your web tier and put it into a specialized, horizontally scalable service that knows the data model." For Git hosting, that specialization unlocks consistent latency, easier scaling, and multi-region replication — at the cost of more moving parts and operational discipline.
keyTakeaways: Extract heavy IO work from web processes: a dedicated RPC service (Gitaly) reduced median Git-op latency from ~700–800ms to ~100–150ms in production scenarios.
Use a routing/replication layer (Praefect) to get HA and multi-node reads while preserving write correctness; expect asynchronous replication trade-offs (staleness vs availability).
Measure the right things: latency percentiles, replication lag, and per-node I/O; specialization buys throughput but increases operational surface area.
relatedTopics: Storage tiering & object storage for repositories
Replication patterns and trade-offs (primary/replica, async replication)

concept.title: Backpressure: How to Stop Your Pipeline from Drowning in Data
concept.summary: Backpressure prevents fast producers from overwhelming slower consumers in streaming systems and services. It converts a silent failure mode (memory spikes, high latency, dropped requests) into explicit flow control signals so systems stay stable and predictable.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine a busy highway where cars (data) pour in from on-ramps (producers) faster than the exit lanes (consumers) can handle. Without traffic control, congestion, crashes, and gridlock happen. In software systems the "crashes" look like OOMs, timeouts, long queues, and cascading failures. *Backpressure* is the traffic light that tells upstream systems to slow down or buffer safely.

### An analogy

- Producer = Fast faucet.
- Consumer = Small sink drain.
- Backpressure = Turning the faucet down, adding a buffer bucket, or asking other faucets to stop.

Backpressure makes the system a cooperative citizen instead of a race to exhaust resources.

### How it works (high level)

There are three common patterns to apply backpressure:

1. Pull-based: consumers ask for N items when they're ready (Reactive Streams).
2. Push with feedback: producers push but receive signals to pause/resume (TCP windowing, stream.pause()/resume()).
3. Drop/Bounded-buffer: apply caps and decide to drop, batch, or reject when full (rate-limiting + circuit breakers).

Which you choose depends on guarantees you need (no-loss vs. best-effort), latency sensitivity, and whether producers and consumers can directly coordinate.

### Sequence: typical backpressure signal flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Producer->>Processor: produce(item)
  Processor->>Consumer: forward(item)
  Consumer-->>Processor: not-ready / slow
  Processor-->>Producer: request-slowdown / pause
  Producer-->>Processor: pause-ed / reduce-rate
  Note right of Processor: Buffer up to N items
  Consumer-->>Processor: ready
  Processor-->>Producer: resume
```

### Before / After (real-world impact)

| Metric | Before (no backpressure) | After (backpressure applied) |
|--------|--------------------------:|-----------------------------:|
| Memory usage | Unbounded growth, OOMs | Bounded to buffer size |
| 95th latency | Spikes heavily under load | Stable, predictable latency |
| Error rate | High (timeouts, rejections) | Lower; graceful degradation |
| Throughput | Bursty, unpredictable | Smoother, sometimes lower peak but more useful |

### Implementation examples and patterns

- Pull-based: Reactive Streams / Project Reactor / RxJava. Consumer requests N items; producer emits only that many.
- Node.js streams: stream.highWaterMark plus .pause()/.resume().
- TCP: sliding window and ACKs — classic network backpressure.
- Message brokers: consumer acks and prefetch limits (RabbitMQ prefetch, Kafka consumer pause())
- API level: 429 responses + Retry-After header or HTTP trailers indicating capacity.

### Quick Node.js stream example

```js
const { Readable } = require('stream');

// Producer: pushes fast
const fastProducer = new Readable({
  read(size) {
    while (this.push('x'.repeat(1024)));
  }
});

// Consumer: slow writable (backpressure respected by stream pipe)
const slowConsumer = new (require('stream')).Writable({
  write(chunk, enc, cb) {
    setTimeout(cb, 50); // slow processing
  }
});

// pipe() handles backpressure automatically: producer.pause()/resume() under the hood
fastProducer.pipe(slowConsumer);
```

### Comparison: ways to handle excess data

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|---------|------|------|-------------|
| Pull (Reactive Streams) | Precise flow control, no implicit buffering | Requires both sides to cooperate | Streaming APIs, microservice-to-microservice flows |
| Push + Pause/Resume (Node streams) | Simple, works with many libraries | Can still buffer if not tuned | File/process streams, pipelines |
| Bounded buffer + Drop/Throttle | Simpler to implement, protects memory | Data loss or increased latency | Telemetry, best-effort ingestion |
| Upstream rate-limit (429) | Explicit and interoperable | Adds retry complexity | Public APIs, multi-tenant services |

### Common pitfalls

- Relying on unbounded buffers: leads to OOM when load spikes.
- Ignoring the need for coordination: some producers can't be told to slow down (e.g., third-party SaaS webhooks)—you need ingress queues + rate limits.
- Mixing backpressure models incorrectly: e.g., using pull semantics at one layer and simple drop-at-capacity at another can create confusing behavior.
- Silent drops without metrics: systems that silently drop data will hide upstream failures.
- Using naive retries on 429s without jitter/exponential backoff: causes thundering herd.

### When to use backpressure

- Streaming pipelines (logs, metrics, event processing).
- Microservices that forward requests on behalf of users (protect downstream services).
- File uploads, video streaming, or any long-lived transfer where consumer speed varies.
- Anywhere resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, disk, network) causes cascading failures.

### Design checklist

- Decide your semantics: must you never lose data? If yes, prefer pull/acks and durable queues.
- Bound buffers explicitly and instrument them (size, drops, wait time).
- Expose clear signals: pause/resume, HTTP 429 + Retry-After, or header-based capacity hints.
- Add backoff and jitter for retrying producers.
- Measure: buffer occupancy, time-in-queue, downstream processing time, and dropped count.

### Closing thought

Backpressure turns an implicit failure mode into a visible control loop. It doesn't make systems faster — it makes them resilient. Design for cooperation, measure behavior, and choose the model that matches your failure and loss tolerance.
keyTakeaways: Backpressure prevents resource exhaustion by making consumers control or signal the flow of data.
Choose a model (pull, pause/resume, bounded-buffer) that matches your guarantees and coordination capabilities.
Always bound buffers, instrument them, and avoid silent drops or naive retries that create herd effects.
